title: Reading and Use of English – Part 1
description: Read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap.

content.text: The art of map-making, or cartography, (0) dates back to ancient civilizations that needed to navigate and manage their lands. Early maps were often (1) on clay tablets or papyrus, and they served both practical and symbolic purposes. For ancient mariners, a reliable map was (2) to a successful voyage across unknown seas. However, these early records were often (3) with mythical creatures and imaginary lands, reflecting the limited geographical knowledge of the time. As trade and exploration (4) , maps became increasingly detailed and accurate. The introduction of the compass and the astrolabe allowed cartographers to (5) more precise measurements of distance and direction. By the Renaissance, the production of maps had become a highly (6) craft, with beautifully illustrated atlases being prized by royalty and scholars alike. These maps did more than just show the way; they also (7) the power and ambition of the nations that commissioned them. Today, digital technology has completely (8) the field, but the fundamental goal of cartography remains the same: to help us understand our place in the world. Modern satellite imagery allows for a level of detail that ancient explorers could only dream of, yet the spirit of discovery that drove them persists.

example.explanation: “Dates back” is the standard phrasal verb for historical origins.

questions.explanation: “Inscribed” is the formal term for carving or writing on a hard surface like clay.
“Essential to” is a fixed collocation meaning absolutely necessary.
“Peppered with” is an idiom meaning something is scattered frequently throughout.
“Flourished” is used here for the successful development of trade and exploration.
“Take measurements” is the standard collocation for using measuring tools.
“Skilled craft” refers to a profession requiring specific expertise and training.
“Conveyed” means to communicate a message or feeling indirectly.
“Transformed” means to change the form or character of something completely.
